trips.name: Full Ring Road Circuit
trips.description: Iceland's Route 1 is the backbone of the island, looping 1332km around a country of fire, ice, and endless sky. From the capital you head north through lava fields and turf-roofed churches, swing east past glacier-carved fjords, and return via the iceberg lagoon and volcanic south coast. No single journey gives a more complete portrait of Iceland's staggering variety.
trips.cover_search_query: Iceland Ring Road Route 1 aerial landscape

trips.name: South Coast & Glacier Lagoon
trips.description: Iceland's south coast packs more drama per kilometre than almost anywhere on Earth — thundering waterfalls you can walk behind, a black sand beach guarded by basalt columns, and finally the ethereal blue icebergs of Jökulsárlón drifting silently to the sea. This drive is a crescendo, each stop more jaw-dropping than the last, ending at the little harbour town of Höfn with Vatnajökull's white mass filling the horizon.

trips.name: Golden Circle Classic
trips.description: The Golden Circle is Iceland's most celebrated day-drive — three world-class natural wonders strung together on a tidy loop from Reykjavík. Þingvellir's rift valley is where the Eurasian and North American tectonic plates visibly separate and where Iceland's parliament was founded in 930 AD; Geysir gives its name to every erupting hot spring on the planet; and Gullfoss hurls the Hvítá River into a double-tiered canyon of roaring white water. Kerið's perfectly circular crimson crater makes a vivid final stop before the city.

trips.name: Reykjanes Peninsula & Blue Lagoon
trips.description: Just south of Keflavík airport lies one of Iceland's most geologically raw landscapes — a peninsula of smoking fumaroles, lava fields, and churning Atlantic coastline. The famous Blue Lagoon's milky-turquoise geothermal waters sit improbably amid a black lava desert, while the Reykjanes lighthouse guards the southwesternmost tip of the island above crashing surf. Krýsuvík's sulfur springs and mud pools add an otherworldly finale before you return to Reykjavík.
trips.cover_search_query: Blue Lagoon geothermal pool Iceland milky turquoise

trips.name: Snæfellsnes Peninsula
trips.description: Jules Verne chose Snæfellsjökull as the entrance to his journey to the centre of the Earth, and the glacier-capped volcano does carry an almost magnetic mystique. The peninsula stretches west into the Atlantic like a finger pointing toward Greenland, flanked by lava archways, seal colonies, and the charming fishing town of Stykkishólmur with its coloured houses reflected in a mirror-calm bay. Grundarfjörður's Kirkjufell mountain — perhaps the most photographed peak in Iceland — provides a perfect farewell.

trips.name: Westfjords Explorer
trips.description: The Westfjords are where Iceland keeps its secrets — a deeply incised peninsula of fjords so remote that most visitors never reach them, leaving the roads blissfully empty and the landscapes fiercely pristine. Dynjandi waterfall cascades in a broad bridal-veil fan down a cliff face visible from kilometres away, and the Látrabjarg bird cliffs at Europe's westernmost point host millions of puffins, razorbills, and guillemots. Ísafjörður, the region's charming capital, offers cosy guesthouses and a warm welcome after long days on winding gravel roads.

trips.name: North Iceland Diamond
trips.description: Northern Iceland is where volcanism and geology put on their most spectacular performance: Goðafoss, the waterfall of the gods, arcs in a perfect horseshoe before the landscape transforms into the alien shores of Lake Mývatn with its pseudocraters and bubbling mud pools. Dettifoss — Europe's most powerful waterfall — sends plumes of spray hundreds of metres into the air, while Húsavík has built a global reputation as Iceland's best town for whale watching.

trips.name: Mývatn & the Highlands Edge
trips.description: This compact northern loop delves deep into Iceland's most geologically active corner, where the landscape looks freshly made — because much of it is. Lake Mývatn's eerie lava formations, pseudocraters, and sulphurous hot springs sit beside the Krafla caldera with its vivid acid lake and steaming lava fields barely cooled from a 1984 eruption. The loop ends at Ásbyrgi's forest-filled horseshoe canyon, a Norse legend frozen in basalt.

trips.name: East Fjords Scenic Drive
trips.description: Iceland's east is the quiet counterweight to the tourist-heavy south — fishing villages tucked into the narrows of glacially carved fjords, reindeer grazing on hillsides, and Seyðisfjörður's rainbow-painted street descending to a fairy-tale blue church. Borgarfjörður Eystri rewards the drive with puffin colonies nesting just metres from the road, while the stone egg sculptures at Djúpivogur nod to the region's understated artistic soul.
highlights: Seyðisfjörður rainbow street and blue church
trips.cover_search_query: Seyðisfjörður rainbow road blue church Iceland

trips.name: Landmannalaugar Highlands (4WD)
trips.description: Landmannalaugar sits at the edge of a vast rhyolite mountain range where the hills run red, yellow, green, and purple — colours that seem borrowed from a painter's palette rather than geology. F-roads through black sand deserts and river crossings lead to steaming hot springs where you can soak surrounded by lava, and the Eldgjá volcanic canyon stretching 75km is one of the largest on Earth. This is strictly 4WD-only territory and the reward is proportional to the effort.

trips.name: Kjölur Highland Route (4WD)
trips.description: The Kjölur route (F35) is the most approachable of Iceland's highland tracks, a straight shot across the interior plateau between two glacier massifs with no major river crossings. Hveravellir oasis — geothermal pools sitting improbably in the middle of a wind-scoured desert — is the perfect overnight stop, the sort of place that makes you feel profoundly far from the ordinary world. The route exits near Blönduós and onwards to Akureyri, connecting south to north through Iceland's bare, beautiful heart.
trips.cover_search_query: Kjölur highland route Iceland F35 desolate plateau

trips.name: Reykjavík & South Iceland Weekend
trips.description: This is the definitive Iceland first-timer loop — three days that manage to pack in tectonic plates, erupting geysers, Iceland's most famous waterfall, a curtain waterfall you walk behind, and a black sand beach fringed by basalt sea stacks. The route sweeps southeast from Þingvellir through the Golden Circle before dropping down to the moody south coast, making it the best possible introduction to what Iceland does better than anywhere else on Earth.

trips.name: North Ring Road: Akureyri to Egilsstaðir
trips.description: If you could only drive one section of the Ring Road, many Icelanders would choose this one: east from Akureyri through the volcanic north, past Mývatn's lunar lakes and the thunderous Dettifoss, then south through increasingly remote highland scenery to the East Fjords. The segment ends at Seyðisfjörður, a Norwegian-heritage village of brightly painted timber houses accessible via a sinuous mountain pass, making the journey feel like a reward compounding with every kilometre.
highlights: Goðafoss the waterfall of the gods

trips.name: West Iceland Sagas
trips.description: West Iceland was the heartland of the Icelandic Sagas — Borgarnes' excellent settlement centre brings the medieval texts to vivid life before you head inland to Reykholt, where scholar Snorri Sturluson wrote the Prose Edda in the 13th century. Hraunfossar is a revelation: dozens of small springs emerge directly from a lava field and cascade into the Hvítá River in a curtain of crystal water, while Húsafell's forest and geothermal pool make a perfect overnight stop before the loop back via Stykkishólmur.
highlights: Snorri Sturluson's medieval hot tub at Reykholt

trips.name: South Ring Road: Reykjavík to Höfn
trips.description: The southern stretch of the Ring Road is Iceland's most photographed corridor — a relentless procession of waterfalls, glacial tongues, black beaches, and finally the drifting blue icebergs of Jökulsárlón that photographers stake out at dawn and dusk. The glacier Vatnajökull — Europe's largest — dominates the horizon for much of the drive, sending its fingers down through Skaftafell's hiking trails all the way to the coast. Ending in Höfn with a plate of the local langoustine and Vatnajökull glowing at sunset is one of Iceland's great travel rituals.
highlights: Reynisfjara basalt columns and sea stacks
trips.cover_search_query: Jökulsárlón diamond beach icebergs Iceland sunrise
